Client 2: Yeah, same. And just… make it simple enough that I can follow it without a giant stack of paperwork.

Advisor: On income, Michael, you mentioned the rental—do you think of that as steady year to year, or does it swing a lot?

Advisor: Hi Mary, hi Michael—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I want a clear plan that keeps our obligations covered and still lets us invest aggressively without feeling cash-tight month to month.
Client 2: Yeah, same. And just… make it simple enough that I can follow it without a giant stack of paperwork.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow. Roughly, what’s the household gross income per year right now—before taxes?

Advisor: Perfect.
Advisor: And marital status—how should I note it? Married, remarried, partnered—what’s the best description for you two?
Client 1: Secondly wedded. We both had prior marriages, so that’s the cleanest way to say it.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. That context helps, especially with the obligations you mentioned earlier.
Advisor: Last one in this set: dependents. Do you have anyone financially dependent on you—kids, maybe a parent—how many dependents should we plan around?

Advisor: Okay, 35%. Thanks. Last household item: how do you want to think about our relationship—are you working with me through an RIA, like a registered investment advisor, or through a brokerage channel?
Client 2: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
